observations.recommendation:
1.10.1. Municipal/Project Engineer to:
1.10.1.1. Conduct inspection and ascertain the project
accomplishment status of the PPAs per the Schedule
of PPAs Still in Construction in Progress as of
December 31, 2024 (Annexes H and I) by indicating
the following:
1.10.1.1.1. Percent of project completion if completed
or not completed;
1.10.1.1.2. If not completed, for projects implemented
in CYs 2017 to 2023, submit justification
for slow implementation; and
1.10.1.1.3. If completed, issue a Certificate of Project
Inspection, stating that the completed
project is per the approved Program of
Works, and submit Certificate of Project
Completion signed by the Municipal
Mayor.
1.10.1.2. Submit Project Status of On-going PPAs as of
December 31, 2024, and pictures of the PPAs per the
Schedule of PPAs Still in Construction in Progress
(Annexes H and I);
1.10.2. Municipal Accountant to:
1.10.2.1. Effect the reclassification of PPAs to their
appropriate PPE accounts upon receipt of the
Certificate of Project Completion.

2.11.1. Municipal Accountant to strengthen control on the
disbursement of funds by proper classification of expenses; and
2.11.2. Enhance coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer to
ensure that expenditures are properly classified.

3.11.1. Justify why the LGU was not able to pay the loan amount of
₱3.319 million, representing the principal and interest, which
resulted in the incurred losses of ₱4.554 million as of December
31, 2024;
3.11.2. Record in the books the outstanding loan payable of ₱3.793
million, the remaining balance of the restructured loan as of
December 31, 2024;
3.11.3. File a petition or an appeal with the NHA that the payments,
made per Table I, totaling ₱2.183 million, be applied to the
outstanding restructured loan balance; and
3.11.4. Henceforth, strictly observe the terms and conditions of the
MOA and contract for contracted loans in the future to avoid
unnecessary financial losses.

4.8.1. Revisit the provisions of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated
May 31, 2022, and COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March
14, 2024, on the proper classification and accounting treatment,
records to be maintained, and reports to be submitted
pertaining to semi-expendable properties;
4.8.2. Effect necessary accounting entries to correct the overstatement
of PPE and supplies and materials expenses as well as the
understated semi-expendable property expenses once
recomputation of the accumulated depreciation has been made;
and
4.8.3. Henceforth observe the correct recording and classification of
expenses pertaining to the PPE, Semi-Expendable Properties
and related expenses.

5.6.1. Direct the Municipal Treasurer:
5.6.1.1. To furnish the Municipal Accountant with duly
certified list of RPT due and collectible for the year;
5.6.2. Direct the Municipal Accountant:
5.6.2.1. To stablish RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of
the year using the duly certified list of taxpayers with
the amount due and collectible based on the
RPTAR/Taxpayer's Index;
5.6.2.2. To ascertain the variance of ₱648,390.51 between the
Certificate of Delinquencies and the balances in the
RPT/SET Receivables as reflected in the books; and
5.6.2.3. To adjust the appropriate accounts to close the
RPT/SET Receivable and Deferred RPT/SET fully
(100%), that is, not only the 40%/50% share of the
Municipality.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Disclose properly the details of Prior Period Errors and furnish the Audit Team with supporting documents used to record the same in a JEV;
b. Determine if the Prior Period Error are material to warrant a restatement of the Financial Statements; and
c. Ensure comprehensive disclosure of the Prior Period Errors in the Notes to Financial Statements, adhering to the guidelines outlined in Paragraph 54 of IPSAS 3.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Stop the practice of charging to government funds of projects/activities without an approved/valid appropriations;
b. Require the Municipal Treasurer to return the amount of ₱3.087 million to the 20% DF account; and
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect the appropriate adjustments to correct the charges of the Hold Out of loan.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Submit a repair history form that includes details such as the type of repair undertaken, the date it occurred, and other relevant information to provide guidance and information for the COA, the agency, and other stakeholders; and
b. Establish and maintain a comprehensive repair and maintenance record for every equipment/vehicle owned and/or operated by the Municipality to minimize the frequently and unnecessary expenses on repairs and maintenance.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Inventory Committee to prepare and submit the Approved Physical Inventory Plan, signed by the Head of Agency, to the COA Audit Team at least ten (10) days before the schedule of inventory-taking activities, pursuant to COA Circular 2020-006, to ensure that all planned inventory activities/procedures are efficiently and effectively performed by the Committee members.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:

Disclose properly the details of Prior Period Errors and furnish the Audit Team with supporting documents used to record the same in a JEV;
Determine if the Prior Period Error are material to warrant a restatement of the Financial Statements; and
Ensure comprehensive disclosure of the Prior Period Errors in the Notes to Financial Statements, adhering to the guidelines outlined in Paragraph 54 of IPSAS 3.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

Stop the practice of charging to government funds of projects/activities without an approved/valid appropriations;
Require the Municipal Treasurer to return the amount of ₱3.087 million to the 20% DF account; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect the appropriate adjustments to correct the charges of the Hold Out of loan.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

Submit a repair history form that includes details such as the type of repair undertaken, the date it occurred, and other relevant information to provide guidance and information for the COA, the agency, and other stakeholders; and
Establish and maintain a comprehensive repair and maintenance record for every equipment/vehicle owned and/or operated by the Municipality to minimize the frequently and unnecessary expenses on repairs and maintenance.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Inventory Committee to prepare and submit the Approved Physical Inventory Plan, signed by the Head of Agency, to the COA Audit Team at least ten (10) days before the schedule of inventory-taking activities, pursuant to COA Circular 2020-006, to ensure that all planned inventory activities/procedures are efficiently and effectively performed by the Committee members.

We recommended and Management agreed to create the Inventory Committee composed of adequate members to conduct the physical inventory taking in accordance to 5.2 of the same COA Circular and ensure the submission of RPCPPE.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. Streamline planning and procurement activities of all infrastructure related PPAs to fast track project implementation of all on-going/not started PPAs;

b. Require the Municipal Development Council to:

b.1 Appraise and prioritize socioeconomic development programs and projects; and

b.2  Coordinate, monitor, and evaluate the implementation of development  programs and projects.

c.	Refrain from programming generic PPAs, instead, consider exact PPAs that are specific, doable and supported with Program of Works, the location is free from legal impediments and ready for implementation;

d. 	Strengthen monitoring activities and come-up with realistic/doable project implementation schedule.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Engineer to:

a. 	Justify why there were no actual disbursements for Equipment, though said same PPAs had a projected cost, per POW, for Equipment totaling ₱467,160.00.

b. 	Submit the POWs on seven implemented PPAs totaling ₱862,385.00;

c. 	Submit Sanggunian Bayan Resolution/approved realignments, it there are, for the implementation of two PPAs not specified in the CY’s AIP or in the PY’s Continuing Appropriations.

d. 	Henceforth, strictly implement 20% DF based on approved AIP.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. 	Discontinue the use of the LDRRMF for administrative and operating expenditures; and

b. 	Ensure that all expenditures from the LDRRMF comply with the allowable expenses as prescribed by DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73 and DBM-DILG-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1.

We recommend and Management agreed to require the:

a. 	LDRRMO to coordinate closely with the Municipal LDC in the formulation of a complete and integrated LDRRMP;

b. 	Departments involved to come up with specific risk reduction and management PPAs and to follow-up/monitor the timetable in the implementation of programmed projects/activities; and

c. 	Local Sanggunian to approve only appropriation for PPAs specifically defined in a manner with brief description, location, appropriated amount, proper description of time of implementation for easy reference and guidance.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the MDRRMO:

a. 	Take immediate action to review its implementation processes and identify the reasons for the non-implementation of the PAPs. 

b. 	Develop a clear and comprehensive plan to maximize the use of available DRRM funds and ensure that the intended benefits are fully achieved. 

c. 	Prioritize the implementation of its planned PAPs and to ensure that funds are utilized efficiently and effectively.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

Require the Municipal Treasurer to:

Provide the Municipal Accountant the certified list of RPT Accounts Register/Tax Index for the current year;

b. Strengthen the collection of RPT/SET thru information or post the notice of the dates when the tax may be paid without interest at a conspicuous and publicly accessible place at the city or municipal hall. Said notice shall likewise be published in a newspaper of general circulation in the locality once a week for two (2) consecutive weeks (Section 249 of the same RA); and

c. 	Monitor and assess collection performance not only on the Performance Targets provided by oversight agencies but also on the actual RPT/SET due and collectible for the current year; and

Municipal Accountant to:

Establish and record RPT Receivable and SET Receivable, at the beginning of the year, based on the certified list of RPT Accounts Register/Tax Index for the current year provided by the Municipal Treasurer using the following entries:

Setting-up of RPT/SET Receivable
Real Property Tax Receivable/ Special Education Tax Receivable
	                 xxx.xx	
Deferred Real Property Taxes Income/ Deferred Special Education Tax Income			          xxx.xx

Record properly RPT and SET collections and their corresponding fines and penalties and discounts on prompt and advance payments using the following entries:

Receipt of Payment
Cash in Treasury	            xxx.xx	
Real Property Tax Receivable/ Special Education Tax Receivable			        xxx.x

Recognition of Income and Distribution of Collection (Current Year’s Collection)			
Deferred Real Property Tax Income/ Deferred Special Education Tax Income	             xxx.xx	
Discount on RPT/SET (for prompt payment)	             xxx.xx	
Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax			        xxx.xx
Fines and Penalties			        xxx.xx
Due to LGUs			        xxx.xx
				
For advance payment of RPT/SET (adjusted on the year the tax is due):			
Other Assets	             xxx.xx	
Deferred Credit to Income		                 xxx.xx


Municipal Assessor to:

f. 	Coordinate with the Provincial Assessor for the revised valuation and assessment of real properties in the municipality pursuant to Section 219 of RA 7160 for an adjustment in the RPT and SET.
We recommended and Management agreed to discontinue contracting the services of a private lawyer performing legal services for the concerns and issues of the municipality.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:


   A. Direct the Supply/Property Officer to:


      a. Prepare an updated list of all PPE  items  costing  ₱15,000.00  and
         above;


      b. Prepare/update Property Cards for all  PPE  items  reported  as  of
         December 31, 2021;

      c. Prepare  the  Inventory  and  Inspection  Report  of  Unserviceable
         Property (IIRUP) for all obsolete or unserviceable PPEs.

      d. Reconcile property records with the Accounting Unit;

      e. Submit to the Inventory Committee the updated list as basis in  the
         preparation of working papers for  the  physical  inventory  taking
         activities.


   B. Require the Accounting Unit to:


      a. Update/prepare PPELCs for all PPE items per Accounting records;


      b. Ensure that updated  PPELCs  reconcile  with  the  controlling  PPE
         General Ledger accounts; and

     c. Reconcile property records with the Property Unit.

We recommended and the management agreed to create an Inventory Committee
   composing of adequate number of members, to include one member each  from
   the accounting and property departments of  the  agency  to  conduct  the
   physical inventory taking in accordance to 5.2 of the same COA Circular.

We recommended and the management agreed to:


   a. Require the Supply/Property Officer to explain  the  unreported  eight
      (8) Transport Vehicles in the Inventory Report;


   b. Require the Supply/Property Officer to submit certified machine copies
      of  Official  Receipt  and  Certificate  of  Registration  to  confirm
      ownership and existence;

   c. Prepare individual Property Cards for each Transport Vehicle; and


   d. Issue Property  Acknowledgement  Receipt/Acknowledgement  Receipt  for
      Equipment to establish accountability of said Transport Vehicles.

We recommended that Management:


   1. Streamline planning and procurement activities of  all  infrastructure
      related PPAs to fast track project implementation of all  on-going/not
      started PPAs;


   2. Require the Municipal Development Council to:

      2.a Appraise and prioritize  socioeconomic  development  programs  and
          projects; and


      2.b  Coordinate,  monitor,  and   evaluate   the   implementation   of
          development programs and projects.


   3. Refrain from programming generic PPAs, instead, consider PPAs that are
      specific,  doable  and  supported  with  Program  of  Works,  to  give
      importance to on the choice  of  location  that  is  free  from  legal
      impediments and ready for implementation;


   4. Strengthen monitoring activities  and  come-up  with  realistic/doable
      project implementation schedule.

We recommended and the management agreed to:


   1. Require the Municipal Engineer to justify the excess by ₱1.556 million
      of actual labor cost  over  the  estimated  labor  cost  of  nine  (9)
      implemented PPAs;


   2. Justify why there were no actual disbursements for  Equipment,  though
      said same PPAs had a projected cost, per POW, for  Equipment  totaling
      ₱1.538 million.
   3. Submit legal basis of the 20 implemented PPAs totaling ₱4.354  million
      that were not specified either in the CY’s AIP for 20% DF or  in  PY’s
      Continuing Appropriations.


   4. Submit Sanggunian Bayan  Resolution/approved  realignments,  it  there
      are, for the implementation of 20 PPAs not specified in the  CY’s  AIP
      or in the PY’s Continuing Appropriations.

We recommended and the management agreed to:


   1. Require the LDRRMO to coordinate closely with the Municipal LDC in the
      formulation of a complete and integrated LDRRMP to formulate  detailed
      programs, projects and activities and to follow the timetable  in  the
      implementation of programmed projects/activities; and


   2. Require Local  Sanggunian  to  approve  only  appropriation  for  PPAs
      specifically defined in a manner that the brief description, location,
      appropriated amount, time of implementation  are  described  for  easy
      implementation reference and guidance.

We recommended and the management agreed to:


   a. Require the Municipal Treasurer to return to the GF-DRRM for  CO  from
      TL-DRRM of the TF  the  amount  of  ₱0.578  million  representing  the

We recommended and the management agreed to:


   a.  Require  the  Municipal  Treasurer  to  record  collections  totaling
      ₱248,812.38 in their respective Cash in Treasury Cash Books; and


   b. Henceforth, record collections daily in  the  Cash  in  Treasury  Cash
      Book;

We recommended and the management agreed to:


   A. Henceforth, require the Municipal Accountant:

      a.    to record vaccines to their appropriate inventory accounts;


      b.    to maintain and post to their respective Supplies  Ledger  Cards
        the donated vaccines received; and


      c.    to reconcile with the inventory of vaccines received and on-hand
        to ascertain discrepancies, if any.


   B. Henceforth, require the Supply Officer:

      a.    to maintain  and  post  in  the  Stock  Cards  the  receipt  and
        issuances of donated vaccines received;


      b.    to prepare the Report of Supplies and  Materials  Issued  (RSMI)
        and submit the same to the Accounting for the recording  of  issued
        vaccines supported with the RIS and List of Vaccinated Individuals;


      c.    to take an inventory of vaccines and reconcile  with  the  Stock
        Cards to determine discrepancies, if any.


   C. Require the MHO to reconcile with the PHO the variance of  20  vaccine
      doses received for Astrazeneca;


   D. Require the MHO to report the status and condition of vaccines on hand
      as of December 31, 2021.

We recommended and the management agreed to:


   1. Require the implementing department to fast track  the  implementation
      of four PPAs totalling ₱299,787.25 and to monitor  the  implementation
      of PPAs funded by the national government; and


   2. Direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct  regular  verification  and
      reconciliation  of  unexpended   balances   of   fund   transfer   and
      return/remit balances to source agency.

Require the Inventory Committee to immediately conduct physical inventory of all the PPE and submit to the Audit Team the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) not later than January 31 of the ensuing year; and Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the GSO Designate to maintain the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC) and the Property Cards (PC), respectively, per category of PPE.
observations.reason: Not Implemented – Reiterated in this year’s AAR. The GSO Designate and Municipal Accountant had not maintained the PPELC and PC as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed to submit proof selection criteria and profiling records and list of qualified target beneficiaries as specified in Section 5.7 of the JMC No. 1.
We recommended and the Management agreed that the OIC-Municipal Accountant be required to submit the DVs on time, to enable the Audit Team to timely evaluate and audit the transactions.

We recommended and the Management agreed that any amount spent not allowed under LBC No.125 be refunded and shall form part of the unutilized funds which shall be reverted to the National Treasury after the lifting of the State of Calamity, by a Presidential Proclamation.

Direct the OIC-MA to prepare the required subsidiary records for the receipts and expenditures for BGCM and other Special Accounts in the General Fund; Require the OIC-MA to submit the monthly report on fund utilization and status of implementation of the BGCM, as per LBC No.125, for proper review of the transactions ; Direct the OIC-MA to prepare necessary adjustments to correct the erroneous entries in the LDRRMF.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant conduct regular review of the recorded transactions and reconciliation of the said accounts, and make the necessary adjustments based on the results of the reconciliation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) shall direct the person in charge to conduct the inventory taking and require him to prepare the report and furnish a copy to the Office of the Auditor periodically in compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The Supply Officer was able to conduct inventory of unserviceable PPEs only.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
a. Require the Inventory Committee to immediately conduct physical inventory of
all the PPE and submit to the Audit Team the Report on the Physical Count of
Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) not later than January 31 of the
ensuing year; and
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the GSO Designate to maintain the
Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC) and the Property
Cards (PC), respectively, per category of PPE.

We recommended and the Management agreed to submit proof selection criteria
and profiling records and list of qualified target beneficiaries as specified in
Section 5.7 of the JMC No. 1.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the OIC-Municipal
Accountant be required to submit the DVs on time, to enable the Audit Team
to timely evaluate and audit the transactions.

We recommended and the Management agreed that any amount spent not
allowed under LBC No. 125 be refunded and shall form part of the unutilized
funds which shall be reverted to the National Treasury after the lifting of the
State of Calamity, by a Presidential Proclamation.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
a. Direct the OIC-MA to prepare the required subsidiary records for the receipts
and expenditures for BGCM and other Special Accounts in the General Fund;
b. Require the OIC-MA to submit the monthly report on fund utilization and status
of implementation of the BGCM, as per LBC No. 125, for proper review of the
transactions ; and
c. Direct the OIC-MA to prepare necessary adjustments to correct the erroneous
entries in the LDRRMF.

In view of the following, we recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort to determine the details of the above-mentioned receivable accounts and make the necessary actions to request for write-off if the details are impossible to establish, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
Henceforth, ensure that account balances presented in the FS be supported with corresponding documents.
We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team proofs of ownership of the subject property and facilitate the immediate titling of the land to ensure the proper ownership of the property.

In view of the following, we recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort to determine the details of the above-mentioned receivable accounts and make the necessary actions to request for write-off if the details are impossible to establish, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

In view of the following, we recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal
Accountant to exert extra effort to determine the details of the above-mentioned
receivable accounts and make the necessary actions to request for write-off if the details
are impossible to establish, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated
December 19, 2016. Henceforth, ensure that account balances presented in the FS be
supported with corresponding documents.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant conduct regular review of the
recorded transactions and reconciliation of the said accounts, and make the necessary
adjustments based on the results of the reconciliation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) shall direct the person in charge
to conduct the inventory taking and require him to prepare the report and furnish a copy
to the Office of the Auditor periodically in compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on
the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team proofs of ownership of the
subject property and facilitate the immediate titling of the land to ensure the proper
ownership of the property.

We recommended that Management:
a. Stop the practice of using the Small Value mode of procurement unless the required
procedures are met;
b. Require the BAC members and BAC Secretarial to attend seminars/webinars and
trainings pertaining to R.A. 9184 for updates; and
c. Follow strictly the provisions embodied in Section 54 of RA 9184 and its Revised IRR
pertaining to the Small Value Procurement.

We recommended that Management designate personnel to act as Supply Officer to take charge of the physical inventory of all PPEs and submit to the Audit Team Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE).
observations.reason: due to the absence of personnel who would take charge of the inventory taking.

observations.recommendation: In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Trial Balances and financial statements in accordance with the rules and regulations, and submit these reports to the Auditor regularly. Likewise, reconciliation of the books of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer must be done regularly.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned LDRRMO to strictly comply with Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 on the submission of the Monthly Reports on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF.
We recommended that Management return the unexpended balance of completed projects to the Source Agency concerned to finance other developmental projects.

We recommended that Management directs the personnel concerned to submit all the lacking documents to the Audit Team to ensure the validity of the transaction and to determine the reasonableness of the acquisition cost of the property.

We recommended that Management create a committee to conduct physical inventory of all the PPEs and submit to the Audit Team the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE).

We recommended that Management designate personnel to act as Supply Officer to take charge of the physical inventory of all PPEs and submit to the Audit Team the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE).

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Trial Balances and financial statements in accordance with the rules and regulations, and submit these reports to the Auditor regularly.  Likewise, reconciliation of the books of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer must be done regularly.

In view of the following, we recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort to determine the details of the above-mentioned receivable accounts and make the necessary actions to request for write-off if the details are impossible to establish, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned LDRRMO to strictly comply with Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 on the submission of the Monthly Reports on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF.
We recommended that Management return the unexpended balance of completed projects to the Source Agency concerned to finance other developmental projects.

We recommended that Management directs the personnel concerned to submit all the lacking documents to the Audit Team to ensure the validity of the transaction and to determine the reasonableness of the acquisition cost of the property.
In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant exerts extra effort to effect the necessary adjustments based on the results of the reconciliation.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with Sections 10 and 54.1 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (R-IRR) of R.A. 9184, prohibiting the splitting of contracts and as a general rule, that all procurement should be done through public bidding except in highly exceptional cases.

We recommended that Management monitor the implementation of the programmed PPAs funded out of the 20% Development Fund to ensure that it is optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipality comply strictly with the provisions in Section 2.2 and Sub-sections 2.2.1 and 2.2.3 of COA Circular 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013, to ensure that the public is informed of any subsequent projects that will be implemented to foster transparency and accountability and also to encourage public participation.

Make an arrangement with a government bank for the opening of individual ATM savings accounts by the employees through a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA); and

Adopt the payroll-thru-bank scheme upon approval of the MOA.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant exerts effort to reconcile the balances of the two reciprocal accounts and make the necessary adjustments thereto, to fully eliminate the difference in the account.

We recommended that Management create a committee to conduct physical inventory
of all the PPEs and submit to the Audit Team the Report on the Physical Count of
Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE).

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant exerts extra
effort to effect the necessary adjustments based on the results of the reconciliation.

We recommended that Management
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect immediately the transfer of the
unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRM-MOOE of the previous years, as well
as for the current year to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability-
DRRM” in the Trust Fund books;
b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the corresponding amount from the
General Fund account to the Trust Fund account; and
c. Maintain subsidiary ledgers for transfers of agency’s unutilized DRRMF to the
special trust fund by year of transfer.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with Sections 10 and 54.1 of the
Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (R-IRR) of R.A. 9184, prohibiting the
splitting of contracts and as a general rule, that all procurement should be done through
public bidding except in highly exceptional cases.

We recommended that Management monitor the implementation of the programmed
PPAs funded out of the 20% Development Fund to ensure that it is optimally utilized to
help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental
management outcomes of the LGU.

a. Require the personnel concerned to submit the following documentary requirements
for the payment of honorarium on the implementation of special projects, per COA
Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012:
1. Performance evaluation plan formulated by project management used as
basis for rating the performance of members;
2. Certificate of completion of project deliverables;
3. Special Project Plan – containing information required in the above stated
Budget Circular;
4. Certificate of acceptance by the agency head of the deliverables per
project component; and
b. Analyze and justify the necessity of the services of the organic personnel, otherwise,
stop the payment of honorarium to these personnel.

We recommended that the Municipality comply strictly with the provisions in Section 2.2
and Sub-sections 2.2.1 and 2.2.3 of COA Circular 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013, to
ensure that the public is informed of any subsequent projects that will be implemented to
foster transparency and accountability and also to encourage public participation.

We recommended that Management:
a) Make an arrangement with a government bank for the opening of individual ATM
savings accounts by the employees through a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA);
and
b) Adopt the payroll-thru-bank scheme upon approval of the MOA.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team a copy of all contracts, purchase orders, and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof, within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-01 dated February 12, 2009.
We recommended that Management return the unexpended balance of completed projects to the NGA concerned to finance other developmental projects.

We recommended that Management cause the immediate submission of the required Office Orders/Travel Orders to avoid audit suspensions and henceforth, require the ICO-Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of supporting documents prior to the approval of the claims by the Municipal Mayor.
We recommended that the collecting officers remit daily and intact all their collections to the Municipal Treasurer in accordance with Section 29 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that Management require the ICO-Municipal Accountant to observe proper accounting and recording of transactions, to make proper reclassification of accounts and to monitor and review liquidations to ensure that funds were spent for public purpose only. Likewise, we recommend that the ICO-Municipal Accountant initiate the cancellation of check no. 1543679 in the amount of ₱12,000,00 if still possible or require the refund if the check was already encashed as of date.

a. Refrain from utilizing the SEF for expenditures that were not among those allocated per Local School Board (LSB) Budget so as not to deplete fund essential in the improvement of the educational system; and
b. Prepare the budget pursuant to the provisions of Republic Act (RA) No. 5447, to avoid improper disposition and utilization of the fund.

We recommended that Management integrate specific PPAs in the Annual Investment Plan detailing every projects/activities to be undertaken with their corresponding budget in line with the 10-year ESWMP and prepare and submit accomplishment report to enable the Audit Team to properly monitor and evaluate the program in terms of its sustainability and effectiveness.

We recommended that the SWM Board develop guidelines and mechanics to ensure proper and quality SWM operation and institute measures that could improve its performance and implementation. Likewise, we recommended that the MENRO be appointed to supervise, monitor and take responsibility for the immediate implementation of various SWM programs and activities as scheduled.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned LDRRMO through the LDRRMC and LDC to submit the Monthly Reports on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF certified correct by the Municipal Accountant within the time prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant exerts effort to reconcile the balances of the two reciprocal accounts and make the necessary adjustments thereto, to fully eliminate the difference in the account.

We recommended that Management:
Create a committee to conduct physical inventory of all the PPEs owned by the Municipality and submit to the Audit Team the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE);
Require the Municipal Accountant and the personnel in charge of the inventory to reconcile their records; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to book up unrecorded PPEs at appraised cost, and unaccounted PPE, if any, be verified.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team a copy of all contracts, purchase orders, and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof, within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-01 dated February 12, 2009.
We recommended that Management return the unexpended balance of completed projects to the NGA concerned to finance other developmental projects.

We recommended that Management cause the immediate submission of the required Office Orders/Travel Orders to avoid audit suspensions and henceforth, require the          ICO-Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of supporting documents prior to the approval of the claims by the Municipal Mayor.
We recommended that the collecting officers remit daily and intact all their collections to the Municipal Treasurer in accordance with Section 29 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommend that Management:
Require the OIC Municipal Accountant to submit immediately the September to December 2016 disbursement vouchers, payrolls and other supporting documents;
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to refrain from releasing payments of transactions with incomplete documentations and signatures of authorized signatories; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with Sections 39 and 107 of Presidential Decree 1445 and COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995 requiring all accountable officers to render monthly reports of their transactions in time and mode as set by law and requiring accountable officer to turn-over documents within the prescribed period, respectively.

We recommended that Management:
Reconsider  the payment of employees’ salaries, allowances, fringe benefits and other emoluments through the bank, as a means of improving fiscal management in accordance with Sections 2 and 4(8) of PD 1445, with the expected result of minimizing the attendant risks identified with cash-paid payrolls and easing the effects of manpower attrition;
Make an arrangement with a government bank for the opening of individual ATM savings accounts by the employees through a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA); and
Adopt the payroll-thru-bank scheme upon approval of the MOA.

We recommended that Management require the ICO-Municipal Accountant to observe proper accounting and recording of transactions, to make proper reclassification of accounts and to monitor and review liquidations to ensure that funds were spent for public purpose only.  Likewise, we recommend that the ICO-Municipal Accountant initiate the cancellation of check no. 1543679 in the amount of ₱12,000,00 if still possible or require the refund if the check was already encashed as of to date.

We recommended that Management:
Refrain from utilizing the SEF for expenditures that were not among those allocated per Local School Board (LSB) Budget so as not to deplete fund essential in the improvement of the educational system; and
Prepare the budget pursuant to the provisions of Republic Act (RA) No. 5447, to avoid improper disposition and utilization of the fund.

We recommended that Management integrate specific PPAs in the Annual Investment Plan detailing every projects/activities to be undertaken with their corresponding budget in line with the 10-year ESWMP and prepare and submit accomplishment report to enable the Audit Team to properly monitor and evaluate the program in terms of its sustainability and effectiveness.

We recommended that the SWM Board develop guidelines and mechanics to ensure proper and quality SWM operation and institute measures that could improve its performance and implementation. Likewise, we recommended that the MENRO be appointed to supervise, monitor and take responsibility for the immediate implementation of various SWM programs and activities as scheduled.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned LDRRMO through the LDRRMC and LDC to submit the Monthly Reports on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF certified correct by the Municipal Accountant within the time prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

Require the Municipal Treasurer to review the work of her staff systematically  especially in the preparation of the Report of Collections and Deposits, issuance of Official Receipts and other accountable forms, as well as the remittances to ensure the correctness and completeness of reports; and

Direct the ICO-Municipal Accountant to see to it that the RCDs are correct before posting/recording in the CRJ to avoid understatement/overstatement of collections and deposits.
We recommend that Management formulate PPAs in accordance with laws, rule and regulations.

We recommend that the Municipality comply strictly with Section 2.2 and            Sub-sections 2.2.1 and 2.2.3 of COA Circular 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013, to ensure that the public is informed of any subsequent projects that will be implemented to foster transparency and accountability and also to encourage public participation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the ICO-Municipal Accountant to review the payrolls and effect the necessary adjustments on the deductions of those receiving below P3,000.00,  in the order  of preference and to inform the employee concerned to make proper arrangements with the other lending institutions on repayments so as not to reduce their net take home pay below than what was prescribed under the General Provisions of the GAA of FY 2015.

We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Accountant be prudent in issuing the Certificate of Net Take Home Pay to their employees, which is one of the requisites when applying for a loan, to avoid the granting of loans to those employees who no longer have the capacity to pay.

We recommended the immediate submission of the required supporting documents for the construction of four (4) covered gyms totalling ₱4,551,948.00 to avoid audit suspension.


We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness and completion of supporting documents prior to the approval of the disbursement voucher by the Municipal Mayor, pursuant to Section 4, paragraph 6 of PD 1445.

Require all department heads to prepare a PPMP in consonance with the budget to support the cost estimates of their budget proposal and ensure that the PPMPs shall include only activities crucial to the efficient discharge of its functions; and

Require the BAC to prepare consolidated APP based on the standard form and shall reflect the entirety of the procurement activities of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strive to reconcile the balances of the two reciprocal accounts and make the necessary adjustments thereto, to fully eliminate the difference in the account.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer closely monitor the implementation of infrastructure projects of the Municipality, to ensure that these projects are implemented in accordance with plans and targets.
